**Title:** Validate SellSmart Copilot guidance with DCs

**Why:** Real‑world DC feedback is required to ensure responses are practical and trustworthy.

**Title:** Enable SellSmart Copilot to answer basic furniture discovery questions

**Why:** DCs need immediate value from the copilot to help locate furniture by type or category.

**Done Looks Like:** DCs can ask simple questions like “Where do I find sectionals?” and receive reliable guidance.

**Before:** No AI‑assisted guidance exists for furniture discovery.

**After:** SellSmart Copilot provides usable furniture discovery guidance.

**Title:** Define how SellSmart Copilot should guide DCs to furniture

**Why:** The copilot must respond in a DC‑friendly, actionable way, not with raw system data or vague responses.

**Done Looks Like:** Clear rules for how the copilot responds, including level of detail, phrasing, and handling of uncertainty.

**Before:** No defined behavior standard for guidance responses.

**After:** SellSmart Copilot responses are consistent, practical, and easy for DCs to follow.

**Type of Work:** ☐ Fixing ☑ Improving ☑ Adding

**Tasks included in this story**

* Define response patterns (directions, sections, confirmation prompts)
* Define how uncertainty or incomplete data should be handled
* Establish guardrails for what the copilot should not answer

**Objective**  
Create a private Zendesk Support app that displays a calendar view of a user’s upcoming tickets based on the **Due Date** field.

---

### **Functional Requirements**

* App displays a **calendar UI** showing tickets assigned to the current user
* Calendar events are driven by:

    * Zendesk **system Due Date field** (Task tickets)
    
* Each calendar entry should show at minimum:

    * Ticket ID
    * Ticket subject
    * Due date
    
* Clicking an entry should link back to the Zendesk ticket

---

### **Technical Requirements**

* Build using **Zendesk Apps Framework (ZAF)**
* Use **ZAF SDK** to:

    * Identify the current user
    * Query tickets assigned to that user
    
* Ticket data may be retrieved via:

    * Zendesk Search API or Tickets API
    
* App should be built as a **Zendesk Support app** (sidebar or top bar placement is acceptable)

📚 Reference:

* Zendesk Apps Framework docs: [https://developer.zendesk.com/documentation/apps/](https://developer.zendesk.com/documentation/apps/) [\[developer....endesk.com\]](https://developer.zendesk.com/documentation/apps/)

---

### **Deployment & Access Control**

* App must be uploaded as a **Private App**
* During installation:

    * Restrict access to **Testing group only**
    * No other agents should see or access the app
    
* This allows **leadership review before broader rollout**

📚 Reference:

* Zendesk app group restrictions: [https://zendesk.oapps.io/hc/en-us/articles/360011903200--How-to-restrict-access-to-any-application-for-certain-users](https://zendesk.oapps.io/hc/en-us/articles/360011903200--How-to-restrict-access-to-any-application-for-certain-users) [\[zendesk.oapps.io\]](https://zendesk.oapps.io/hc/en-us/articles/360011903200--How-to-restrict-access-to-any-application-for-certain-users)

---

### **Acceptance Criteria**

* ✅ App loads successfully for users in Testing group
* ✅ App does NOT appear for non‑Testing users
* ✅ Calendar correctly reflects ticket due dates
* ✅ Links navigate to correct Zendesk tickets
* ✅ No production‑wide visibility before approval

    * Case Iniator takes call and creates ticket OR gets round robin a emailed ticket

    * Parent Ticket is the first ticket made for communication witht he client. Once finish being built it will be sent assigned to Quintina/Tiffany who will assign to a CCS for ALL future communications
    * The child ticket that was created by the CI is by each vendor involed on the sales order
    * This child ticket is what also generates the Case in Netsuite using the barcodes filled out by the CI
    * Then that is assigned to micheal weimer who assigns to a Vendor Communication Specialist(VCS)
    *  If there is any issues or missing information like photos for example it will be returned to the CI
    * If escalated it will go to supervisor depending on role so CI=don gibson CCS=Quintina/Tiffany, and VCS= Micheal Weimer.

### User story

* As a Case Initiator, when I receive a call or an emailed ticket, I want to create a ticket so that I can manage the request and resolution effectively.
* As a Case Initiator, when I generate a child ticket, I want it to create a Case in Netsuite so that I can track vendor communications accurately.

### Context

The issue involves cloning the CCS Dashboard to create a new dashboard specifically for Case Initiators. This dashboard will help Case Initiators manage their tickets and communications more effectively.

### Acceptance criteria

* Clone the CCS Dashboard for Case Initiators.
* Tag all Case Initiators on their agent accounts with “ci” and “case_initiator__ci_\_”.
* Set the Custom role filter to CI.
* Ensure the workflow for Customer Service is integrated:

    * Case Initiators can create tickets from calls or emailed tickets.
    * The first ticket must be the Parent Ticket for client communication.
    * Child tickets must be generated for each vendor involved in the sales order.
    * Child tickets should create Cases in NetSuite using barcodes filled out by the Case Initiator.
    * Assign generated Cases to Michael Weimer, who will then assign them to a Vendor Communication Specialist (VCS).
    * Handle any issues or missing information by returning tickets to the Case Initiator.
    * Escalate cases to supervisors based on roles (CI to Don Gibson, CCS to Quintina/Tiffany, VCS to Michael Weimer).
    
* Adapt the dashboard to ensure that not all CCS Dashboard reports are relevant for Case Initiators.

**Subject:** VC Round Robin

 

Hey Jaylon,

 

I'm not so sure what the logic is on the VC round robin.  The Zendesk default is the next ticket goes to the person that was assigned a ticket the longest time ago, but that doesn't appear to be happening.  On the first day, Hanna received 3 tickets across a span of 8 assigned and Amy received 3 across a span of 11 assigned.  Yesterday I assigned Hanna 3 tickets first thing, then the 4th round robin ticket went to her as well.  Amy received 3 of the first 5 round robin tickets for the day.  Throughout both days there are several instances of someone receiving multiple tickets before someone else gets one.  There was only a single instance of 7 consecutive tickets going to 7 different people(Kelly Doss was off both days).  

 

Total Assigned 3/11 - 3/12

 

|  | Manual | R-R | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Amy | 0 | 10 | 10 |
| Chris | 0 | 6 | 6 |
| Darcey | 1 | 5 | 6 |
| Hanna | 7 | 7 | 14 |
| Jasmine | 0 | 6 | 6 |
| Kelly | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| Tammy | 4 | 4 | 8 |
| Terri | 1 | 5 | 6 |

 

 

It seems Amy and Hanna are the ones getting a majority of the tickets.  I've manually assigned several to Hanna, but the system should account for that and skip over her. I haven't manually assigned any to Amy and she's received significantly more round robin tickets than anyone else. 

 

Can you look at this and see why the tickets aren't being distributed in a balanced manner?  I currently have Amy, Kelly and Chris offline to get caught up.

 

Thanks

assigned.summary: 2/12 Meeting: Enhance Vendor Promotion Pricing Visibility
assigned.description:
* Validate how tariff pricing is currently calculated and surfaced in vendor promotions.
* Update SellSmart outputs to consistently include tariff‑adjusted pricing so sales reps have full cost visibility during customer conversations.

* Review recent examples of context confusion in multi‑request conversations.
* Identify patterns where intent or scope is being lost and define expectations for conversation state persistence and follow‑up handling.

* Audit current SellSmart responses to identify where NetSuite links are missing.
* Define clear criteria for when a NetSuite link is required and ensure consistent inclusion across all applicable result types.

Here's a recap of the suggestions from our last three sessions:

* Wallpaper vendors and their promotions
* Saved searches for vendor promos
* Lead times for upholstery and casegoods
* Insights from past sales reps
* Consider finding the most recent sales order for reference
* "Good, better, best" price points
* Vendors with specific products

SellSmart currently relies on manually uploaded CSVs (vendor contacts, extranet credentials, gallery advocate data, lead times), which require frequent updates and become stale quickly. We should explore long‑term automation options:  
**(1)** replacing CSVs with a Boomi‑backed synchronous API for live vendor data from NetSuite, or  
**(2)** building an automated service that periodically exports NetSuite saved searches / SuiteQL queries and refreshes the CSVs in Copilot.  
The goal is to eliminate manual updates and improve accuracy and maintainability.

The SellSmart Update Review Meeting reinforced the importance of increasing visibility and maintaining a high level of trust in SellSmart by delivering transparent, dependable results. 

Key themes included ensuring **NetSuite links are present on all applicable outputs** to support verification and confidence, addressing **context confusion in multi‑request conversations**, and incorporating **tariff pricing into vendor promotions** so sales reps have complete pricing visibility at the point of use. 

Additional focus areas included participating in **new‑hire training** to better understand real workflows and identify opportunities for SellSmart to provide meaningful assistance, as well as reviewing how **consignment vendor entries** are stored to prevent data ambiguity. 

Overall, the discussion emphasized building a tool that integrates cleanly into daily workflows and can be relied upon without friction, confusion, or loss of trust.

assigned.summary: Stella Bot on front page during Off Hours
assigned.description:
### User story

* As a client, when I visit the main page after hours, I want to interact with the Stella bot so that I can get answers to my questions without waiting for a live agent.
* As a member of the Client Relations team, when I receive inquiries after hours, I want the Stella bot to handle Q&A so that I can focus on more complex issues during regular hours.

### Context

Stella bot will be featured on the main page after hours to assist with inquiries. The Client Relations team, led by Quintina, usually manages live chat inquiries. During off-hours, the bot will provide answers based on provided documents.

### Acceptance criteria

* Identify questions the bot cannot currently answer using the provided documents.
* Create articles for questions that the bot cannot answer.
* Build flows for specific tasks, such as building a room.
* Ensure that questions leading to a ticket are directed to Case initiators.
* Clearly mark tickets for presales.
* Focus on deflecting ticket creation by answering FAQs and guiding users to the appropriate pages.
* Include quick options for pricing/suggestions and scheduling a visit.
* Remove the quick option for the website.

### Other information

The Client Relations team has provided two documents:

1. A list of inquiries they have received recently.
2. A cheat sheet of questions, answers, and usual flow.

### Summary

A user inquired about finding forms for the trade account from their profile page. The bot incorrectly responded with the Account Details flow instead of providing navigation instructions.

### Context

The issue arises when users seek specific information about their trade account. The bot's response does not align with the user's request, leading to confusion.

### Acceptance criteria

* The bot should provide clear instructions for navigating to the trade account forms from the profile page.
* The response should not redirect users to the Account Details flow when they ask about forms.

assigned.description:
## Architecture Overview

This is a Next.js 15 application with a modern React architecture focused on AI-powered product matching. The project uses:

### Core Framework & Structure

* **Next.js App Router** (using the `app/` directory structure)
* **TypeScript** for type safety
* **Server-side rendering** with API routes for backend integration

### UI Framework & Styling

* **shadcn/ui** component library (evident from components.json)
* **Tailwind CSS** for styling
* **Radix UI** primitives as the foundation (extensive usage in pnpm-lock.yaml)
* **Lucide React** for icons

### Data & Search Integration

* **Python backend integration** via API routes for embedding generation
* **FAISS vector search** integration for similarity matching
* **Real-time search engine** with both text and image search capabilities

SellSmart currently relies on manually uploaded CSVs (vendor contacts, extranet credentials, gallery advocate data, lead times), which require frequent updates and become stale quickly. We should explore long‑term automation options:  
**(1)** replacing CSVs with a Boomi‑backed synchronous API for live vendor data from NetSuite, or  
**(2)** building an automated service that periodically exports NetSuite saved searches / SuiteQL queries and refreshes the CSVs in Copilot.  
The goal is to eliminate manual updates and improve accuracy and maintainability.

The SellSmart Update Review Meeting reinforced the importance of increasing visibility and maintaining a high level of trust in SellSmart by delivering transparent, dependable results. 

Key themes included ensuring **NetSuite links are present on all applicable outputs** to support verification and confidence, addressing **context confusion in multi‑request conversations**, and incorporating **tariff pricing into vendor promotions** so sales reps have complete pricing visibility at the point of use. 

Additional focus areas included participating in **new‑hire training** to better understand real workflows and identify opportunities for SellSmart to provide meaningful assistance, as well as reviewing how **consignment vendor entries** are stored to prevent data ambiguity. 

Overall, the discussion emphasized building a tool that integrates cleanly into daily workflows and can be relied upon without friction, confusion, or loss of trust.

reported.description:
## Architecture Overview

This is a Next.js 15 application with a modern React architecture focused on AI-powered product matching. The project uses:

### Core Framework & Structure

* **Next.js App Router** (using the `app/` directory structure)
* **TypeScript** for type safety
* **Server-side rendering** with API routes for backend integration

### UI Framework & Styling

* **shadcn/ui** component library (evident from components.json)
* **Tailwind CSS** for styling
* **Radix UI** primitives as the foundation (extensive usage in pnpm-lock.yaml)
* **Lucide React** for icons

### Data & Search Integration

* **Python backend integration** via API routes for embedding generation
* **FAISS vector search** integration for similarity matching
* **Real-time search engine** with both text and image search capabilities
